speaker: interviewer
text: Sol Einhorn. June 11-- what did I say? It's June 11, 1996. And we're speaking with Sol Einhorn. And we just got a lot of the background. Sol, we were talking about your life in your town, Moisei. Can you pronounce it for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Moisei, Moisei.

speaker: interviewer
text: Moisei. And you were in your town pretty late into the war, weren't you? In the early '40s, you were still there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about when did the trouble start for you for your family in Moisei, in about what year?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the-- it started in 1944. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] 1944 [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] came an order [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] to the town that we shall get ready, and pack up. And we going to go into the next town, all together, all Jewish people. And anybody will obey, they have a list from every kid, and everyone in the family. Anybody will be missed, the parents will be shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did that happen from one day to the next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: That happened just from one day to the next? There were no changes in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was-- before this happened, and overnight, about two weeks before, they took away from the town-- from the village rather-- they took away the rabbi, and the leadership from the village. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My-- my grandfather was the president of the town many years before. They didn't take him. They took the president of the town, what was the president in 1944. And they took a couple of intellectuals, about half a dozen people, overnight, just took them out from their houses. And they never-- we never heard-- never knew anything. Up to this, never knew what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: And two weeks later, the news-- how did they give the news? I mean, we had-- we didn't have radios. Or we didn't have newspapers. A man from the [INAUDIBLE], from the town, had a drum.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he went every two, three hours, [INAUDIBLE] drummed. And the people came out. And he read out from the paper, about the law, and what we would do, and what going to be done. And came out the day before, we shall get ready. And tomorrow morning, there's going to be wagons. And as much as we can carry, everybody can take along covers, and so on, only brought as much as a person can carry, and put on the wagon. And we'll go into next town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Up until that point-- that was pretty late, 1944. And you were already a teenager. Did you remember hearing about other things that were going on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes, we had-- people were taken away from the village to-- they called it [GERMAN], so to work on to the front for the Nazis, for the Hungarians. People in 28, from 25 on, 21 years and up till 35, they took these men, Jewish people, to a labor camp, labor. And one-- two of them ran away and came back. And he came back to town a year or six months before we were evacuated. And a matter of fact, he was a very religious man. And he took a [NON-ENGLISH] and a [NON-ENGLISH]. And he came back. And he said that I saw with my-- saw with my eyes, they took gasoline, and put on barracks, and put on fire people-- they locked them in and burned them, Jewish people. And when they came back, it was so far-fetched that nobody-- nobody could believe it. And they said, he's off his [NON-ENGLISH]. And the other guy was a-- he was a businessman. The other guy was a butcher. And he really, when he came back, he made himself crazy. And he told his stories. And he became crazy. They sent him back, or somehow he escaped. I don't know exactly. Now, we could-- we heard about it. We couldn't-- we couldn't believe it. And we could not-- just, I mean, that human beings will do a thing like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, nobody could believe it. Did you ever meet any refugees from other countries that had escaped, like from Poland or other lands?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yours was just a small town, and you didn't have much interaction with the other people?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not much.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so then you eventually had to leave. Like, what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we-- next morning, we were talking, mother and father thought maybe the bigger boys should run away, and go away to the mountains. We had mountains. And here, the law is if, you know, that anybody, parents will be shot. As many kids are going to miss-- anybody missed from the family-- we were 11, so many people are going to killed, starting with the parents. So there is what-- so very, very close family. And we packed up. And we went to next town.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole family?

speaker: interviewee
text: The whole family?

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you take with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We took covers. We had some silver, [NON-ENGLISH], what we used the candles for Shabbas, dishes, and only what we were able to carry, and whatever food we had available.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't take books, or pictures, or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: Books, no, very little, very little. My father took some [NON-ENGLISH], a couple of-- took the [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: So where did you go? You went to the next town?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to next town in [NON-ENGLISH]. And there, matter of fact, we stayed in the same area where my-- in the same house. I had an uncle there. And that whole area, half of that town became the ghetto. And all the towns and villages, all the Jews were brought in there. And we stayed together in the town, uncle and aunt with their kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I remember is, well, four to six weeks.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened? Excuse me. Do you remember the name of the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH]. And the town, we were talking for escaping, escaping. And my brother-- older brothers than me, Mende. He was two years older than me. And he was tall, strong guy. And he and the other-- he had a partner in the lumber business. And he and other boys in that age, four of them, somehow at night made themselves papers, somewhere in the ghetto, and escaped, and ran out of the ghetto, dressed in the-- you know, the gentiles were wearing a different costume of uniform-- white clothes, instead of regular material clothes. They made their own clothes. And they left. And I never heard from them. I never from those four guys again, till after the war. And I-- and so they caught them while-- in the-- in the town on the mountains. And they brought them in to the concentration camps. And they-- all of them disappeared. They're not alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were in that town. And then what happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that town, they had transports. The train, they gave orders that [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] one transport, they loaded up to Auschwitz. We didn't know in that time where, what.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean they just came to the house and-- how did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, they gave an order, the town there, we had a Jewish community. And they said a certain amount, the names from the ghetto. And they went with the first transport they had, loaded up the train. I don't know-- remember-- many many wagons, cattle wagons. Ad women and children, grandparents, and so on. We went on the second transport. They had-- from [NON-ENGLISH], from this ghetto, they had three transports of train, loaded up the trains. And we went into the train. I went in my father and mother, my two grandfathers, and-- and an uncle, two uncles, and two aunts, children, and other people, packed in like sardines. And it was very, very terrible, terrible.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you any idea how many people there were?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't-- I don't know. We were very, very packed, crowded. And I remember one thing in the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: My father, may he rest in peace, from the train was-- we were riding. And you had to move-- you know, had to make-- move your body, you know? So he-- he became an inventor, I would call it today.

speaker: interviewee
text: He went ahead in a corner of the train, and with knives-- we didn't have tools-- he made a hole in the-- in the wagon, and put up a piece of curtain. And anybody had to go, went-- they went there. And the smell still was unbearable in the-- from the kids, and the things. I remember once-- once they opened the wagons, and a free-- free area, where they gave us water. You know, we run over for water, and they gave us water. And it was spring. The trees were blooming. The grass was green in the area where the train stopped. And we went for-- for 10, 15 minutes. And we went out, and got water. And we went back into the wagons.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you say wagons, can you describe it a little better? Is it like a cattle car? Was it open? When you wagon, was it open?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, cattle car-- a cattle car, and the doors were going in, and out. And you just lock it from outside. And the only thing what you had in that wagon, you have two small windows on top-- one on one end from the wagon, and one on the other end from the wagon.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- if we want to try to imagine, when you say wagon, you mean like a car in a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon/

speaker: interviewer
text: It would be like a car in a train?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many such cars there were in the train, or how many wagons?

speaker: interviewee
text: If I looked-- if I looked back and I look, I couldn't see the end of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were about 16 at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I beg your pardon? Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were 16.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you any idea how long the trip was?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would-- five, six days, almost a week, five, six days.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were you able to keep time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Day and night.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you remember, you counted it? And you could see through the windows if it was day or night? When you made your stops, did you see any people from the towns?

speaker: interviewee
text: I might remember day and night. I remember the mornings. And they used to put on [INAUDIBLE]. [SOBBING] I Among my grandfather [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] had [INAUDIBLE] all day. [SOBBING]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you want some water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you want some water?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it's all right. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] OK, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember, when you made your stop for water, did you see any people from the towns that you passed through?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we have the people on the wagon. I saw the people-- the other people from the town, from my town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, when you got off, you said when you got off, did you pass-- like, what did you pass? When did you stop? Was it like a country area? Was it in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we stopped the car-- empty field, empty fields. [INAUDIBLE] prepared there, empty field. I mean, you couldn't hide. You could see miles and miles, empty fields, greenery, and small little trees.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what month it was? You said it was spring. Do you remember what month it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: April.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you mentioned that you stopped for water. What did you about food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food, whatever we had, and had taken along, what we had in the bags. We had bread. We had-- we had food. We had a little bit of food, we had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have enough for the whole trip?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we didn't have. We shared. Everybody had-- we shared. And we made-- everybody--

speaker: interviewer
text: You rationed it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rationed it, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was guarding the trains? And when you got off, who was watching you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The German officers, the German-- the Nazis, the soldiers. They were with their machine guns outside, and all on the line, lined up, every wagon [INAUDIBLE] two, three machine guns.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when the-- when the Jews got out of the cars, what did the soldiers do? Were they cruel? Or were they quiet? Or they just stood there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Stood there, and we just ordered-- they had their own people order what we should do, get out, and get water, and get right away back in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they hit or beat anybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [GERMAN] with sticks or something, nothing out of line.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you arrive? What camp did you arrive in?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what date it was? It was still in April?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was still in April.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did you arrive? Was it the night, or the day? Do you remember that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We arrived in middle of the night, or in the evening. But they didn't open the wagons till daylight in the morning, in the morning.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed in the trains in Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the train in Auschwitz-- we didn't know where Auschwitz, not Auschwitz. We didn't know what it is, or where we are. Now, we know. Now, we talk about it. We didn't know. In the morning--

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got off the train, what did you see?

speaker: interviewee
text: They opened the wagons. We jump-- we stayed up. And everybody tried to look. We couldn't see the window to look where-- where we are, or what it is. I remember when they opened the wagons, my mother, she rest in peace, asked a Polish young man who spoke Yiddish, working on the platform there. And one of them, number one, I remember got hurt from a capo. He was with stripes. And now I know, it was a capo in a striped uniform. He had a striped uniform. That he had a band. And he hit them over the head. And he said [GERMAN], watch one thing. They shouldn't hit you on the head. Be [INAUDIBLE], be fast. They shouldn't hit you on the head.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, yeah-- and the only thing I remember I saw-- what I remember, I saw a big chimney, big, black smoke. And my mother asked this young man, the Polish, where are we? What is this? What is this here? What are we here? So she-- don't you see the [INAUDIBLE], they're burning bodies, people. They're burning people. And so the other woman said [NON-ENGLISH], something wrong with him. It couldn't penetrate that they're burning people, women and children. No, you couldn't imagine, right. So when you got off the train, were there soldiers there that told you what to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were soldiers-- the German soldiers, they had machine guns, or guns, machine guns, all over. And-- and they told us where they will lead us. They told us what to do. Women, we should leave all the packages. And women that side, and the men on this side, and they separated us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the packages were left, something you could carry it in the hand, or some-- I don't remember exactly carrying anything. And I remember one thing came in front of-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] he's called. Now, I know he was Mengele, his name. And there was big, two dogs, to offer-- to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Gestapo, to Germans with the machine guns. He was in the middle with white gloves. Their boots were shining, like all mighty, who knows what. And he just-- he came in front of-- on the line, come to our line. We were five, six in the line, mostly families. We were staying together. And the women were right next to us, not far in the line too. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] So I remember the mother send over to the father, one of my brothers, little brothers, six, seven years old. There was a brother and sister, twins, and to ask father if they should give out the twins. They asking, twins shall come out. And the [INAUDIBLE] went around, twins should go out. And he sneaked it over before we got in the front. And father said, no, positively not. It was a boy and a girl.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. I don't understand. Your father said no to what?

speaker: interviewee
text: To give-- they were asking for twins, any twins between the-- between the people, to give them out, to come out. And my mother said-- asked father what she shall do. And he said, no. This is what I remember. And then we walked further in the front, in front of Mengele. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] the father, my father had my two grandfathers [INAUDIBLE], my mother's father and his father. And he was-- my father was a tall man, a strong man. He could have survived, could of, maybe yes, maybe. I don't know. But-- and he says-- he was holding the-- holding the two grandparents of my-- Mengele said to him-- me and my brother. I had an older brother and me. We go to to right, or the left, whatever, to the side. And my father, he made also he should go. And he said, what he should do with the-- then he said, no, go with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: All three?

speaker: interviewee
text: All three, and that's the way he went to the-- the extermination, what I understand today. I didn't know to--

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was your father? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 43, 44.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you know for sure that it was Mengele?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was nobody else in there. I was told. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I wouldn't know-- I-- after the concentration camp, we talked about him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever see pictures of him that you could recognize him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I saw pictures. I don't think I can recognize him today. Today, [INAUDIBLE] maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the men, the father and the grandfathers were taken. What about your mother and the grandmothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: They went-- went also to the-- with the children.

speaker: interviewer
text: They went with the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Children, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they go, to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one-- one sister went also to work-- one-- two years or a year and a half younger than me. She didn't-- she worked in Auschwitz in the camps there. She didn't survive. And I had an older brother to me, also in Auschwitz, two years older. He didn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, under what circumstances didn't he survive? Was he taken to the gas chambers?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the labor camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Auschwitz with him. And they-- in Auschwitz, they-- I was in Auschwitz a short time, only two, three days. And the one-- I didn't do anything there. Just in the morning, appellplatz, cleaned the streets, swept two or three days. And then they-- one morning, they kept us on the appellplatz, counting, counting. We were thousands of people there. And one day, they counted. I was arm to arm with my brother. And one day, they counted. And my brother was next to my right and. And they counted me up. I had to go to bed. And my brother remained there. And that's all I ever heard and ever saw.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were only in Auschwitz for a few days?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And most of your family stayed in Auschwitz too?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you given any kind of identifying number or anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we went in-- I got a striped uniform, and pants, [NON-ENGLISH], a hat, and a jacket, and a t-shirt, or whatever, and an underwear or something. And [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] that's about-- with a number-- a number with a yellow--

speaker: interviewer
text: A star?

speaker: interviewee
text: A yellow star right on the corner from the number. And the number I remember 3642.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still remember that number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Still remember that number. I'll never forget.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we're going to stop this tape now. And we'll be back with the next tape.
